attributes.stem:
An 86-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic in a residential aged care facility (RACF). She moved into the facility three weeks ago, having previously lived at home alone. The staff report that she has been exhibiting challenging behaviour overnight, often found wandering with verbal agitation and arguments with staff when redirected back to her room.

She has a medical history of vascular dementia, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and osteoporosis. Her regular medications include amlodipine, metformin, and alendronate. She does not take any other regular medications.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Neurological examination reveals no focal deficits. She is oriented to person but not to time or place.

The staff are requesting a prescription for a regular night-time antipsychotic to manage her behaviour.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, the most appropriate management is to implement non-pharmacological interventions. This approach is considered first-line for managing behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD), which include wandering, agitation, and arguments as described in this patient. The recent move to the residential care facility three weeks ago may be a contributing factor to her behavioural changes, highlighting the importance of non-pharmacological approaches to help her adjust to her new environment.

Prescribing regular night-time risperidone or other antipsychotics should be avoided as a first-line approach in patients with dementia, and should only be used in severe cases if non-pharmacological approach is unsuccessful, or there is imminent or high risk of harm to the patient, caregivers or other residents. Antipsychotics are associated with an increased risk of stroke and death in elderly patients with dementia, as well as medication related adverse effects.

Starting a course of sertraline, an antidepressant, is not appropriate as the first step. While depression can contribute to BPSD, there's no clear indication of depression in this case, and antidepressants are not recommended as a first-line treatment for BPSD.

Commencing regular paracetamol is not indicated as there's no mention of pain, which can sometimes contribute to behavioural issues in dementia. However, a thorough assessment for pain should be part of the overall evaluation as it is a common contributor to delirium and BPSD.

Increasing frequency of GP visits alone is unlikely to address the immediate behavioural issues, although regular review is important for ongoing assessment and management, particularly as this patient has recently moved in to the RACF.

Non-pharmacological interventions for BPSD can include: establishing consistent routines, providing engaging activities during the day to reduce night-time restlessness, ensuring a calm and familiar environment, addressing any unmet needs (hunger, thirst, need for toileting), and staff education on dementia care. It's also crucial to identify and address any underlying causes of behavioural changes, such as infections, pain, or medication side effects.

In this case, helping the patient adjust to her new living environment should be a key focus. If these measures are ineffective, a careful, time-limited trial of pharmacological treatment might be considered, but only after a thorough risk-benefit analysis.
learningPoints: Non-pharmacological interventions are the first-line approach for managing behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD).
Antipsychotics should be avoided in patients with dementia due to increased risk of stroke and death.
Identifying and addressing underlying causes of behavioural changes is crucial in managing BPSD.
references: James IA, Gray K, Moniz-Cook E, Lee K, Reichelt K, Richardson J. Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia: a new framework for holistic understanding and non-pharmacological management. BJPsych Advances. 2022;28(1):11-20. doi:10.1192/bja.2021.12. Available online at ; https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bjpsych-advances/article/behavioural-and-psychological-symptoms-of-dementia-a-new-framework-for-holistic-understanding-and-nonpharmacological-management/168FE7A4CF18530BFC3E376D01E19716

Burns, K.*, Casey, A-N.* & Brodaty, H. (2023). A Clinician’s BPSD Guide 
2023: Understanding and helping people experiencing changed behaviours and psychological symptoms associated with dementia (2nd ed.). Centre for Healthy Brain Ageing (CHeBA), Sydney, University of NSW, 2023. Available online at ; https://www.hammond.com.au/hubfs/DSA/DSA%20Resources%20Library/pdfs/A%20Clinician_s%20BPSD%20Guide%202023.pdf?__hstc=108267805.a4f2ad8aa8cf211f1ce8f2d616fe0cba.1729051394842.1729051394842.1729051394842.1&__hssc=108267805.2.1729051394842&__hsfp=1464332008
